name: Tschäggättä
city: Lötschental Valley (villages: Wiler, Ferden, Kippel, Blatten)
dates: From day after Candlemas (February 3) to eve of Ash Wednesday; parade evenings (except Sundays) throughout that period. Major events: traditional Tschäggättä parade and Lötschentaler Fasnachtsumzug in Wiler.
description: One of the most unusual Alpine Carnival traditions in Europe, found in the remote Lötschental valley of Valais, Switzerland. The Tschäggättä are wild, frightening figures who wear old clothes turned inside out, sheep or goat skins hanging front and back, burlap trousers, mittens, and jute-wrapped legs — and most distinctively, enormous hand-carved wooden masks (Arvenholz / pine-like wood) that are oversized, grotesque, and sometimes brightly painted. Masks may be adorned with cow teeth, horns, and goat-hair wigs. Each Tschäggätta carries a wooden staff (Schtäkkun). Unlike organized carnival groups, the Tschäggättä generally do not parade in groups — they roam the streets individually, tossing soot at passersby and maintaining strict anonymity. Every evening except Sundays during Carnival, they run through the valley's four villages chasing anyone outdoors. They also participate in a large Lötschentaler Fasnachtsumzug parade in Wiler. The tradition developed during centuries of the valley's isolation; legends attribute the costume to 'Schurten thieves' — small, stocky men who lived in the woods. First official mention is in a 19th-century church chronicle. Today women and girls may also participate, though it was historically exclusively young men and bachelors. The Lötschentaler Museum in Kippel houses a fine collection of historical masks.
